content:
Act as a senior Kubernetes platform engineer.
I am learning how Kubernetes Operators work.
Explain:

1. CRDs
2. Controllers
3. Reconciliation Loop
4. controller-runtime

using charts and practical examples.
Assume I know basic Kubernetes but have never written an Operator.

Welcome to the control plane! Moving from deploying applications on Kubernetes to writing Operators is a fantastic transition. It is like moving from driving a manual car to writing the software for a self-driving system.

An Operator is essentially a Kubernetes Controller that understands human-level, domain-specific knowledge about a particular application (like a database).

Letâ€™s break down the four core concepts you need to know, using a practical example of building an Operator to manage a PostgreSQL Database.

1. Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs)

The Concept: Out of the box, Kubernetes only understands native resources: Pods, Deployments, Services, ConfigMaps, etc. If you ask Kubernetes to create a PostgresCluster, the API server will reject it because it doesn't know what that is.

A CRD (Custom Resource Definition) is how you teach the Kubernetes API server new vocabulary. It is a schema definition. Once registered, users can create Custom Resources (CRs) based on that definition.

Practical Example: You write a CRD that defines a PostgresCluster. It specifies that this resource must have fields like replicas, storageSize, and version.

Once the CRD is applied, a user can write a simple YAML file (the Custom Resource) like this:

apiVersion: db.example.com/v1
kind: PostgresCluster
metadata:
  name: billing-db
spec:
  replicas: 3
  version: "14"

Note: The CRD is just data at rest in etcd. It tells Kubernetes how to store and validate the YAML, but it doesn't actually do anything. To make it do something, you need a Controller.

2. Controllers

The Concept: If the CRD is the "What", the Controller is the "Who". A Controller is a background process (usually running in a Pod in your cluster) that actively watches the Kubernetes API for changes to specific resources.

When you combine a Custom Resource with a Controller that understands the domain logic of that resource, you get an Operator.

Practical Example: Your PostgresCluster Controller watches the API server. When a user runs kubectl apply -f billing-db.yaml, the Controller gets a notification: "Hey, a new PostgresCluster was created!" The Controller then looks at the YAML and says, "I see they want a Postgres database with 3 replicas. I know how to do that. I need to create a StatefulSet, a Service, and a Secret for the passwords."

3. The Reconciliation Loop

The Concept: This is the brain of Kubernetes. Controllers do not react to one-off events (like a traditional webhook); instead, they constantly compare the Desired State (what the user asked for in the YAML) with the Actual State (what is physically running in the cluster).

When these two states drift apart, the loop takes action to reconcile them. This is known as level-triggered logic (like a thermostat), rather than edge-triggered logic (like a light switch).

Practical Example:

  1. Desired State: User wants replicas: 3.
  2. Actual State: Controller checks the cluster. Due to a node crash, there are only 2 Postgres Pods running.
  3. Reconcile: The loop fires. The Controller calculates the diff (3 - 2 = 1) and issues a command to the API server to create 1 new Pod.
  4. Repeat: The loop runs again. Desired: 3, Actual: 3. The loop does nothing and goes back to sleep.

4. controller-runtime

The Concept: In the early days of Kubernetes, writing a Controller was brutal. You had to manually interact with raw API mechanics: setting up Informers (watchers), Workqueues (to prevent processing the same event twice), leader election, and caching.

controller-runtime is an open-source Go library provided by the Kubernetes project (specifically SIG-Machinery). It abstracts away all the painful, boilerplate distributed-systems plumbing so you can focus entirely on writing the business logic of your Reconciliation Loop. Tools like Kubebuilder and Operator SDK use controller-runtime under the hood.

Key Components it provides:

  • The Manager: The central engine. It handles starting your controllers, setting up metrics endpoints, and managing Leader Election (ensuring only one instance of your Operator is actively making changes if you deploy multiple replicas for high availability).
  • The Cache: Every time your operator asks Kubernetes, "How many Pods exist?", it would overwhelm the API server if it made a network call. controller-runtime maintains a local, lightning-fast, in-memory cache of the cluster state that is kept in sync automatically.
  • The Client: A pre-configured tool used to easily Read (from the cache) and Write (directly to the API server) Kubernetes objects.
  • The Reconciler Interface: This is the only part you actually have to write. You implement a single Go function that looks like this:
// This is the heart of your Operator using controller-runtime
func (r *PostgresReconciler) Reconcile(ctx context.Context, req ctrl.Request) (ctrl.Result, error) {
    // 1. Fetch the PostgresCluster instance (Desired State)
    var db dbv1.PostgresCluster
    if err := r.Get(ctx, req.NamespacedName, &db); err != nil {
        return ctrl.Result{}, client.IgnoreNotFound(err)
    }

    // 2. Check actual state (e.g., look for associated StatefulSets)
    // 3. Compare and Act (Create/Update/Delete resources)

    // 4. Return success! (The loop will wait until something changes again)
    return ctrl.Result{}, nil
}

Summary

You teach Kubernetes a new word using a CRD. You write a Controller that understands what that word means. The Controller runs a continuous Reconciliation Loop to ensure reality matches the user's request. And you build the whole thing using the controller-runtime library so you don't have to reinvent the wheel.

Explain how a Kubernetes Operator processes a new Custom Resource from creation to status update.

Show the entire lifecycle.

Include API Server interactions.

Let's trace the exact path of a Custom Resource (CR) from the moment you hit enter in your terminal, all the way to the Operator updating its status.

Understanding this lifecycle is what separates people who just use Operators from engineers who can debug and architect them. Kubernetes is an API-driven, event-based system, and the Operator sits right in the middle of that traffic.

Here is the complete lifecycle of a Custom Resource, focusing heavily on how it interacts with the Kubernetes API Server.

Phase 1: The Request (User to Kube-APIServer)

Everything in Kubernetes starts with an API call.

  1. The POST Request: You run kubectl apply -f my-db.yaml. kubectl translates this into an HTTP POST request to the API Server at the endpoint defined by the CRD (e.g., /apis/db.example.com/v1/namespaces/default/postgresclusters).
  2. Validation & Admission: The API Server checks if the user is authenticated and authorized (RBAC). It then runs the payload against the CRD's OpenAPI schema to ensure the YAML is valid (e.g., replicas is an integer). It may also pass through Mutating/Validating Admission Webhooks.
  3. Storage: The API Server persists the raw object into etcd.
  4. Response: The API Server returns a 201 Created back to kubectl.

Key Takeaway: At this point, the Operator hasn't done anything yet. The CR is just data resting safely in etcd.

Phase 2: Observation (Kube-APIServer to Operator)

Your Operator is constantly running in the background, utilizing an Informer (part of controller-runtime). Informers maintain a long-running HTTP connection to the API Server, "watching" for events.

  1. The Watch Event: Because a new CR was saved to etcd, the API Server pushes an ADD event down the open watch connection to your Operator.
  2. Cache Sync: The Operator receives the JSON payload of your new PostgresCluster. Instead of doing work immediately, it updates its local, in-memory cache to reflect this new reality.
  3. Enqueueing: The Informer pushes a "Key" (usually just namespace/name, e.g., default/my-db) into a Workqueue.

Why a Workqueue? If the user updates the CR 10 times in one second, the queue deduplicates these events into a single key. This prevents the Operator from running 10 overlapping reconciliation loops and creating race conditions.

Phase 3: The Reconciliation Loop (The Operator's Brain)

A worker thread picks up the default/my-db key from the Workqueue and triggers the Reconcile function.

  1. The GET Request (Cache): The Operator executes Client.Get() for default/my-db. To save API Server load, controller-runtime serves this request directly from the local cache.
  2. The Diff Calculation: The Operator analyzes the CR's Spec (Desired State: 3 replicas) and queries the cluster for associated resources (Actual State: 0 StatefulSets found).
  3. The POST Requests (Act): The Operator realizes it needs to build the database. It sends POST requests directly to the API Server to create native resources:
    • POST /api/v1/namespaces/default/secrets (for credentials)
    • POST /apis/apps/v1/namespaces/default/statefulsets (for the pods)
    • POST /api/v1/namespaces/default/services (for networking)

Phase 4: The Status Update (Closing the Loop)

Once the Operator has dispatched the commands to create the underlying resources, it needs to tell the user what happened.

  1. The /status Subresource: In Kubernetes, you should never modify the Spec of an object programmatically; the Spec belongs to the user. Instead, Operators write to a special endpoint called the Status Subresource.
  2. The PATCH Request: The Operator sends an HTTP PATCH request to /apis/db.example.com/v1/namespaces/default/postgresclusters/my-db/status.
    • It updates fields like Phase: Creating or ReadyReplicas: 0/3.
  3. Re-queue (Optional): The Reconcile function returns. If the database takes 5 minutes to boot, the Operator might return ctrl.Result{RequeueAfter: time.Second * 10} to check back later, or it will simply wait for a new watch event when the underlying StatefulSet finally becomes ready.

Visualizing the Lifecycle

To help solidify this mental model, I have generated an interactive visualization of the architecture. You can step through the flow of data from the User, through the API Server, into the Operator's internal mechanics, and back out.

Give an example of a Database Operator.

Show what the YAML looks like and explain what happens internally after I run:

kubectl apply -f database.yaml

Letâ€™s look at a concrete example using a MySQL Operator.

When you run a database like MySQL in production, you don't just need Pods. You need a primary node, read replicas, persistent storage, automated backups, and configuration files. A good MySQL Operator handles all of this infrastructure automatically.

Here is what the YAML for a Custom Resource (let's call it MySQLCluster) looks like:

apiVersion: mysql.oracle.com/v2
kind: MySQLCluster
metadata:
  name: prod-db
  namespace: default
spec:
  instances: 3
  version: "8.0"
  storage:
    size: 50Gi
  backup:
    schedule: "0 2 * * *" # Every day at 2 AM

What happens internally after you run kubectl apply?

  1. The API Server Accepts the Request: kubectl sends this YAML to the Kubernetes API server. The API server validates it against the MySQLCluster CRD schema. Since it is valid, it saves this Desired State into etcd.
  2. The Operator is Notified: The MySQL Operator, running in a pod somewhere in your cluster, has an Informer watching the API server. It receives an event: "A new MySQLCluster named 'prod-db' was created."
  3. The Reconcile Loop Begins: The Operator fetches the full YAML from its local cache. It reads the spec and says: "The user wants 3 instances of MySQL 8.0, with 50Gi of storage, and daily backups."
  4. The Operator Calculates the Diff: It checks the cluster for existing resources tied to prod-db. It finds none. The Actual State is 0, but the Desired State is 3.
  5. The Operator Acts (The Magic): This is where the Operator executes its domain-specific knowledge. It doesn't just create random pods. It makes a series of API calls to the Kubernetes API server to create native resources:
    • StatefulSet: It creates a StatefulSet with 3 replicas. It configures the first pod to initialize as the Primary, and the next two to initialize as Read-Replicas.
    • PersistentVolumeClaims (PVCs): It requests three 50Gi block storage volumes from your cloud provider so the database data survives pod restarts.
    • Services: It creates a prod-db-primary Service (for writes) and a prod-db-read Service (to load balance across the replicas).
    • Secrets: It generates secure root passwords and stores them as Kubernetes Secrets.
    • CronJob: It creates a Kubernetes CronJob scheduled for 0 2 * * * to run an automated backup script.
  6. Status Update: The Operator checks the status of the StatefulSet. Once the Pods are running and healthy, it patches the MySQLCluster status field to State: Ready.

Explore the Operator Logic

To truly understand how an Operator works, you need to see how it maps your simple Custom Resource inputs into complex native Kubernetes objects.

Use the interactive tool below to adjust the Desired State of our MySQLCluster. As you change the inputs, watch how the Operator's Reconcile loop instantly rewrites the underlying native Kubernetes infrastructure to match.

Explain the Open Programmable Infrastructure (OPI) project.

What problems does it solve?

Explain its architecture.

Focus on:

- CRDs
- Controllers
- Vendor abstraction
- Existing DPU Operator

Use architecture diagrams.

The Open Programmable Infrastructure (OPI) project is an open-source initiative under the Linux Foundation. To understand OPI, you first have to understand the hardware shift happening in modern data centers: the rise of the DPU (Data Processing Unit) and IPU (Infrastructure Processing Unit).

Modern servers are offloading infrastructure tasksâ€”like software-defined networking, storage virtualization (NVMe-oF), and zero-trust securityâ€”away from the main CPU and onto dedicated smart cards (DPUs/IPUs).

The Problem OPI Solves

Vendor Lock-in and Fragmentation.

Before OPI, if you wanted to deploy DPUs in your data center, you faced a nightmare:

  • If you bought an NVIDIA BlueField DPU, you had to write your infrastructure code using NVIDIAâ€™s proprietary SDK (DOCA).
  • If you bought an Intel IPU, you used Intelâ€™s SDK (IPDK).
  • If you bought an AMD Pensando DPU, you used their specific P4 toolchain.

You could not write a single piece of software (like a firewall or a Kubernetes CNI) and run it on all of them. OPI was created to solve this by defining standard, vendor-agnostic APIs. It allows the software ecosystem to build against a single abstraction layer, regardless of the physical silicon sitting in the server.

OPI Architecture: Vendor Abstraction

OPI relies on a standard set of gRPC APIs. Instead of talking directly to the hardware, your software talks to the OPI API.

  1. The OPI API Server runs on the DPU.
  2. It exposes standardized gRPC endpoints for Networking, Storage, and Security (e.g., "Create an IPsec tunnel," "Provision an NVMe volume").
  3. Vendor Plugins (The Shim Layer): Underneath the OPI API Server, vendors provide a translation plugin. This plugin takes the standard OPI gRPC call and translates it into their proprietary hardware instructions (DOCA, IPDK, etc.).

Kubernetes and OPI: The DPU Operator

Managing a fleet of servers with DPUs adds massive complexity. You have to boot the DPU OS, configure its networking, provision firmware, and set up tenant isolation. This is where Kubernetes and the DPU Operator come in.

The existing DPU Operator ecosystem (heavily driven by Red Hat and hardware vendors in the Kubernetes Network Plumbing Working Group) aims to treat the DPU as just another Kubernetes resource.

Here is how the concepts map to the Operator pattern:

1. CRDs (Custom Resource Definitions)

To manage DPUs, the cluster needs new vocabulary. The Operator introduces CRDs to represent the state and configuration of the infrastructure cards.

  • DpuCluster or DpuProvisioning: Defines the desired state of a fleet of DPUs (e.g., which firmware version they should run).
  • DpuNodeState: Discovers and reports the actual hardware capabilities of a specific DPU on a specific node.

Example DpuCluster CRD:

apiVersion: opi.example.com/v1alpha1
kind: DpuCluster
metadata:
  name: prod-dpu-fleet
spec:
  dpuImage: "registry.example.com/dpu-os:v1.2"
  networkingType: "ovn-kubernetes"
  tenantIsolation: true

2. Controllers

The DPU Operator consists of multiple controllers running in the Kubernetes control plane. Their reconciliation loop looks like this:

  • Observe: The Controller reads the DpuCluster CRD to see the desired firmware and network setup. It uses a daemon running on the host nodes to query the actual state of the local DPU via standard OPI management interfaces.
  • Diff: It compares the running DPU OS version against the spec.dpuImage.
  • Act: If the DPU needs an update, the Controller coordinates with the host node to flash the new image over the PCIe interface, reboot the card, and re-establish the network plumbing, entirely hands-off.

3. How they integrate

By combining the DPU Operator (which handles the lifecycle and plumbing of the card in K8s) with the OPI APIs (which handle the data plane rules), you get a fully programmable infrastructure. The K8s Control Plane talks to the Operator, the Operator configures the host and DPU via OPI standards, and the OPI shim translates that to the silicon.

As a platform engineer, choosing how to orchestrate DPUs (Data Processing Units) usually comes down to a philosophical and architectural debate: do you want an open, vendor-agnostic ecosystem, or do you want deep, proprietary optimization for a specific piece of silicon?

The OPI DPU Operator (driven by the Linux Foundation and Red Hat) represents the open-source, vendor-agnostic approach. The NVIDIA DPF (DOCA Platform Framework) Operator is NVIDIA's highly specialized, enterprise-grade solution built exclusively for their BlueField hardware.

Here is the technical comparison of how they operate under the hood:

Architecture & Lifecycle Comparison

FeatureOPI DPU OperatorNVIDIA DPF Operator
ResponsibilitiesVendor-agnostic DPU lifecycle management and network attachment (like OVN-Kubernetes). Focuses on treating DPUs as standard K8s resources.End-to-end BlueField orchestration. It flashes firmware, manages a secondary K8s control plane specifically for DPUs (often via Kamaji), and deploys DOCA microservices.
CRDsDpuOperatorConfig, DpuCluster, DpuNodeStateDPFOperatorConfig, DPUSet, DPUCluster, DPUService, DPUServiceChain, DPUFlavor
ControllersUses a generic host-daemon controller. The controller communicates with the DPU via standard OPI gRPC APIs to trigger provisioning and plumbing.Highly specialized controllers. They manage Kamaji (for tenant control planes) and integrate with Argo CD to push complex networking applications directly to the DPU.
ReconciliationReconciles the desired DPU OS image and network plumbing state against the generic hardware state reported by the node.Reconciles specific BlueField configurations (BFB images), ensures DPUs join the secondary cluster as worker nodes, and reconciles complex Service Function Chaining (SFC).
Vendor AbstractionHigh. Designed to work across NVIDIA (DOCA), Intel (IPDK), and AMD (P4) by relying on a vendor-specific shim layer beneath the OPI standard.None. Tightly coupled to NVIDIA BlueField silicon, the proprietary DOCA software stack, and NVIDIA-specific networking (Host-Based Networking).
Status UpdatesReports standard lifecycle phases (e.g., provisioning, flashed, ready) back to the primary Kubernetes API server's /status endpoints.Provides deep, hardware-specific telemetry (via DOCA Telemetry Service), tracking intricate service chain statuses across both the management and DPU clusters.

The Bottom Line

If your infrastructure relies heavily on NVIDIA BlueField and you want to leverage advanced Service Function Chaining (SFC) and proprietary DOCA features, the NVIDIA DPF Operator is the necessary route. It essentially turns your DPUs into their own distinct Kubernetes cluster that sits underneath your application cluster.

If you are building a multi-vendor hardware strategy (mixing Intel, AMD, and NVIDIA) and want to manage infrastructure offloading through a single, unified, open-source API layer, you should look toward the OPI DPU Operator.

Work Log: Integrating NVIDIA DPF into the OPI Ecosystem

Date: July 5, 2026 Role: OPI Operator Maintainer / Senior Platform Engineer Objective: Architect NVIDIA hardware support into the open-source OPI DPU Operator while maximizing the reuse of NVIDIAâ€™s existing, proprietary DPF (DOCA Platform Framework) Operator.

Here is the design breakdown of the potential architectural paths, evaluated against our core engineering metrics.

Approach 1: Operator Composition (The Facade Pattern)

Concept: The core OPI Operator acts as a high-level manager. We keep our unified OPI CRD (DpuCluster) as the single point of entry for the user. When our controller's reconciliation loop detects that the target hardware is an NVIDIA BlueField DPU (likely via Node Feature Discovery labels), we do not attempt to write custom hardware logic. Instead, our OPI Operator dynamically generates and applies the NVIDIA-specific CRDs (DPFOperatorConfig, DPUSet, DPUFlavor).

We rely entirely on the pre-installed NVIDIA DPF Operator to watch those new CRDs and do the actual heavy lifting (flashing firmware, configuring Kamaji, setting up Host-Based Networking).

  • Maintainability (High): We avoid writing or maintaining proprietary NVIDIA DOCA logic. We only maintain a YAML translation map between our CRD and theirs.
  • Complexity (Medium): We have to manage K8s OwnerReferences (linking the DPF CRs to the parent OPI CR) to ensure garbage collection works correctly if a user deletes the DpuCluster.
  • Kubernetes Best Practices (Excellent): This is the classic "Operator of Operators" pattern, commonly seen in tools like the Operator Lifecycle Manager (OLM).
  • Scalability (High): We inherit the native scaling capabilities of the DPF Operator.
  • Failure Handling (Complex): This is the main friction point. Our controller must constantly watch the Status subresource of the generated DPF CRDs, interpret NVIDIA's specific error codes, and translate them back into generic OPI status messages for the user.

Approach 2: Provider Extension Pattern (The Cluster API Model)

Concept: We restructure the OPI ecosystem into a core/provider model. The core OPI Operator manages the abstract desired state but executes nothing. We build a separate, lightweight controller named opi-nvidia-provider. This provider is dedicated to watching the core OPI CRDs and acting as the bridge to the DPF Operator APIs.

  • Maintainability (Very High): Complete decoupling. The core OPI codebase remains pure and entirely vendor-agnostic. Changes to the NVIDIA DPF Operator only require updates to the isolated provider repository.
  • Complexity (High): This requires significant upfront architectural work. We have to define a strict K8s webhook and API contract so the core operator and the provider can communicate.
  • Kubernetes Best Practices (Perfect): This perfectly mirrors standard, mature Kubernetes ecosystem patterns like Cluster API (CAPI), which uses separate infrastructure providers (CAPA for AWS, CAPZ for Azure) to translate generic cluster requests into vendor-specific API calls.
  • Scalability (Excellent): The core operator and the provider scale completely independently.
  • Failure Handling (Clean): The provider has a single responsibility: mapping DPF hardware errors to the standardized OPI error schema before patching the core CRD status.

Approach 3: Co-existence & Handoff (The Annotation Model)

Concept: We design the OPI Operator to explicitly ignore nodes with NVIDIA DPUs. Using a Mutating Webhook, when a user submits a generic request, we identify if the target is NVIDIA and simply append a specific annotation or label (e.g., opi.linuxfoundation.org/managed-by: dpf). The user is required to manually install and interface with the NVIDIA DPF Operator for those specific nodes.

  • Maintainability (Low): While it requires almost no code on our end, it pushes the architectural debt onto the end user.
  • Complexity (Low): Trivial to implement.
  • Kubernetes Best Practices (Poor): This fractures the user experience. It violates the foundational goal of OPI: providing a single, unified abstraction layer for all programmable infrastructure.
  • Scalability (N/A): Relies entirely on the standalone deployments.
  • Failure Handling (Fragmented): The user has to debug failures across two completely different Operator ecosystems with different logging standards and CRD structures depending on which physical rack a workload lands in.

Architectural Ranking & Decision

  1. Provider Extension Pattern
  2. Operator Composition (Facade)
  3. Co-existence & Handoff

Rationale: The Provider Extension Pattern is the definitive choice for the long-term health of the project.

While the Facade Pattern (Approach 1) is faster to implement in a single sprint, tightly coupling the core OPI Operator to NVIDIA's proprietary CRDs introduces long-term brittleness. By enforcing a Provider Extension Architecture (Approach 2), we maximize the reuse of the DPF Operator without compromising the vendor-agnostic integrity of the OPI project. It establishes a highly scalable plugin system, setting a clean precedent for when we inevitably need to integrate AMD Pensando or Intel IPU providers in the future. Approach 3 is discarded as it fails to solve the user's pain point of vendor lock-in.

Approach 1â€”the Higher-Order Orchestrator or CRD Delegation patternâ€”is the most elegant and Kubernetes-native way to integrate the Open Programmable Infrastructure (OPI) Operator with NVIDIA's DOCA Platform Framework (DPF).

Instead of writing custom Go code to interface with NVIDIA's proprietary APIs, the OPI Operator treats the DPF Operator as a "black box" microservice. It manages NVIDIA hardware simply by writing Kubernetes YAML (Custom Resources) for the DPF Operator to consume.

Here is a detailed breakdown of how this architecture operates in practice.

Components

The architecture relies on standard Kubernetes control plane mechanics, divided into distinct layers:

  • The Kubernetes API Server (etcd): The central state store and communication hub. The Operators never speak directly to each other; they only speak to the API Server.
  • The OPI Operator (The Meta-Controller): The top-level orchestrator. It registers standard, vendor-agnostic OPI Custom Resource Definitions (e.g., DpuCluster, DpuNodeState).
  • The NVIDIA DPF Operator (The Executor): The low-level, vendor-specific controller. It registers NVIDIA-specific CRDs (e.g., DPUCluster, DPUSet, DPUService) and handles all proprietary logic.
  • The Hardware (NVIDIA BlueField DPUs): The physical silicon executing the compiled BFB images and Host-Based Networking configurations.

Responsibilities

Strict separation of concerns is the primary advantage of this approach.

OPI Operator Responsibilities:

  • Provide a single, unified API for platform users to request DPU infrastructure, regardless of the underlying vendor.
  • Detect the hardware type present on the cluster nodes.
  • Translate the generic OPI parameters (e.g., OS version, network isolation requirements) into the specific schema required by the DPF DPUSet or DPUCluster resources.
  • Inject OwnerReferences into the generated DPF resources to ensure proper Kubernetes Garbage Collection.

NVIDIA DPF Operator Responsibilities:

  • Watch for its native DPUSet/DPUCluster resources.
  • Execute proprietary hardware lifecycles: trigger PXE boots, push BFB OS images over the PCIe bus via RShim, and initialize the DOCA telemetry stack.
  • Manage the secondary "DPU tenant cluster" (often via Kamaji) required by NVIDIA's architecture.

Communication

Because both operators share the same Kubernetes control plane, communication is strictly declarative and asynchronous. 1. Top-Down Provisioning: The OPI Operator creates, updates, or deletes a DPF Custom Resource via standard HTTP POST/PUT/DELETE calls to the Kubernetes API server. 2. Level-Triggered Reactivity: The DPF Operator's Informers receive a watch event from the API server regarding the new DPF resource and begin reconciliation. 3. No Direct Coupling: The OPI Operator pod does not need network access to the DPF Operator pod. It does not invoke gRPC or REST APIs on the DPF controller directly.

Status Synchronization

Status flows bottom-up in a "bubbling" pattern.

  1. Hardware to DPF: The DPF node agent reports hardware state (e.g., "Firmware Flashed Successfully") back to the DPF Operator.
  2. DPF to API Server: The DPF Operator patches the /status subresource of its DPUSet object (e.g., ReadyReplicas: 1/1, Condition: Provisioned).
  3. API Server to OPI: Because the OPI Operator created the DPUSet (and set an OwnerReference), it maintains a watch on it. The OPI Operator receives an update event when the DPF status changes.
  4. OPI Translation: The OPI Operator reads the proprietary DPF status, translates it into the standardized OPI status schema, and patches the /status of the top-level DpuCluster resource. The user sees a clean, vendor-agnostic "Ready" state.

Failure Recovery

Delegation allows for robust, isolated failure domains.

  • Hardware Failures: If a BlueField card fails to boot, the DPF Operator attempts its internal retry logic. If it permanently fails, it sets a Condition: Failed on the DPUSet. The OPI Operator reads this failure and surfaces it to the user. The OPI Operator can be programmed to attempt a recovery by deleting the DPUSet (triggering a teardown) and recreating it to force a fresh provisioning cycle.
  • DPF Operator Crash: If the DPF Operator pod crashes (OOM, panic), the hardware keeps running. Once Kubernetes restarts the DPF pod, it resumes reconciliation. The OPI Operator is completely unaffected by this crash.
  • Orphan Mitigation: Because the OPI Operator sets OwnerReferences on everything it creates, if a user deletes the parent DpuCluster resource, the Kubernetes Garbage Collector will automatically delete the child DPUSet resources. The DPF Operator will then see the deletion timestamp and cleanly wipe the BlueField hardware before the object is fully removed from etcd.

Assume we have chosen a CRD delegation architecture where the OPI Operator creates and manages NVIDIA DPF Custom Resources instead of configuring hardware directly. Critically review this design as if you were a maintainer of the OPI project. Identify weaknesses, operational challenges, lifecycle management issues, and scalability concerns. Do not suggest implementation details yetâ€”focus on architecture.

While the CRD delegation patternâ€”acting as a higher-order orchestratorâ€”is elegantly Kubernetes-native on paper, Day 2 operations in a production environment reveal significant architectural fractures. As a maintainer of an open-source standard like OPI, tying our control plane to a proprietary downstream operator introduces severe systemic risks.

If we adopt this architecture, we must address the following critical flaws.

1. API Coupling and Dependency Rot (Weaknesses)

By generating DPF Custom Resources, the OPI Operator becomes strictly coupled to the API schema of the NVIDIA DPF Operator.

  • Schema Evolution Friction: OPI is an open standard that moves at the pace of the community. DPF is a proprietary product that moves at the pace of NVIDIA's release cycles. If NVIDIA updates their API (e.g., deprecating a field in DPUSet v1alpha1 and moving to v1beta1), the OPI Operator will suddenly start generating invalid YAML, causing catastrophic provisioning failures across the cluster. We are forcing OPI to constantly chase DPF's API tail.
  • The "Lowest Common Denominator" Problem: If OPI defines a rich standard for networking, but DPFâ€™s CRDs only support a subset of those features, the OPI Operator is forced to silently drop requested features or block their implementation. We lose control over our own feature velocity.

2. The Opaque Box and Abstraction Leaks (Operational Challenges)

Treating the DPF Operator as a "black box" assumes that the box behaves predictably. In a distributed hardware system, it rarely does.

  • Opaque Error Bubbling: When BlueField hardware fails (e.g., a BFB image corruption during a PXE boot), the DPF Operator will update its DPUSet status. However, it will likely use a proprietary, undocumented NVIDIA error code. The OPI Operator is supposed to present a vendor-agnostic interface to the user. It now has to parse these raw, vendor-specific string errors to make sense of them, completely shattering the vendor abstraction.
  • Split-Brain Risk: What happens if a cluster administrator uses kubectl to manually edit the generated NVIDIA DPUSet directly, bypassing the OPI DpuCluster? The DPF Operator will reconcile the admin's changes, but the OPI Operatorâ€™s cache will show that reality no longer matches its desired state. The OPI Operator will fight the admin, continuously overwriting their changes, leading to an infinite reconciliation war.

3. Lifecycle and State Management Flaws

Relying entirely on Kubernetes API primitives like OwnerReferences for lifecycle management is dangerous when spanning different operational domains.

  • Cross-Namespace Limitations: By design, Kubernetes OwnerReferences strictly prohibit cross-namespace ownership. If the OPI Operator's DpuCluster is a cluster-scoped resource (or lives in an opi-system namespace), but NVIDIA requires DPUSet resources to be deployed in specific tenant namespaces, native Garbage Collection breaks entirely. We will leave orphaned DPUs running across the cluster if an OPI resource is deleted.
  • Orphaned Out-of-Band State: If the OPI Operator deletes a DPUSet, it assumes the DPF Operator will cleanly wipe the hardware. If the DPF Operator pod is down, or if the DPF Finalizers hang due to a network partition, the OPI Operator has no way to force a hardware reset. The node becomes fundamentally unmanageable from the OPI layer.

4. Control Plane Amplification (Scalability)

This architecture is inherently noisy and exponentially increases the load on the Kubernetes API Server.

  • etcd Object Doubling: For every single OPI resource in the cluster, we are generating at least one (often more) DPF resources. In a massive data center with 10,000 DPUs, we are doubling the amount of infrastructure data stored in etcd, accelerating database bloat.
  • Reconciliation Storms: Status synchronization in this architecture causes a "double-hop" write penalty.
    1. Hardware state changes.
    2. DPF Operator writes an HTTP PATCH to the API server (Update 1).
    3. The API server broadcasts a watch event to the OPI Operator.
    4. The OPI Operator wakes up, translates the status, and writes an HTTP PATCH to the API server (Update 2). In a fleet reboot scenario, this doubles the API server latency and network traffic, risking control plane throttling.

Summary

While delegating to DPF saves us from writing hardware-specific Go code, it forces us into a corner where we own the responsibility for cluster state without having actual authority over the hardware execution. We become a brittle translation proxy, vulnerable to API changes, opaque errors, and API Server bottlenecks.
For each of the identified weaknesses, propose architectural mitigations while keeping the overall architecture unchanged. Focus on Kubernetes Operator best practices.

To harden this CRD delegation architecture without changing the fundamental topology, we must rely on advanced Kubernetes controller mechanics. By moving beyond basic CRUD operations and utilizing modern API machinery, we can build a resilient meta-operator that mitigates the risks of acting as a higher-order orchestrator.

Here is the architectural mitigation strategy for each identified weakness.

1. Mitigating API Coupling and Dependency Rot

The core risk is that hardcoding the NVIDIA DPF Go structures binds the OPI Operator to NVIDIAâ€™s release cycle and schema changes.

  • Adopt the unstructured.Unstructured Client: Instead of importing the DPF Operator's Go modules (api/v1alpha1/dpuset_types.go), the OPI Operator should interact with the Kubernetes API using the dynamic client and unstructured JSON/YAML maps. This breaks the compile-time dependency on NVIDIA's codebase.
  • Implement an Adapter/Translation Registry: Build an internal translation layer within the OPI Operator that detects the available DPF API version on the cluster (via the Discovery Client). If the cluster runs v1alpha1, it routes the payload through a specific schema adapter; if it detects v1beta1, it uses another.
  • Feature Gating for the LCD Problem: To solve the "lowest common denominator" issue, implement a Capabilities API or Feature Gates in the OPI Operator. If a user requests a generic OPI networking feature that the underlying DPF version does not support, the OPI Operator can immediately reject the DpuCluster CR via a Validating Admission Webhook with a clear error, rather than failing silently downstream.

2. Mitigating the Opaque Box and Abstraction Leaks

We must prevent manual administrative interference and translate vendor-specific errors into standardized vendor-agnostic states.

  • Server-Side Apply (SSA) and Field Managers: To prevent the "split-brain" reconciliation war where an admin manually edits the generated DPF resource, the OPI Operator must use Kubernetes Server-Side Apply. By claiming "ownership" of specific fields (as a Field Manager) in the DPUSet, the API server will explicitly handle merge conflicts. If an admin tries to modify a field managed by OPI, the API server will reject it unless the admin forces the change, in which case the OPI Operator can cleanly step back or raise an alert rather than fighting in an infinite loop.
  • Error Translation Mapping: Do not blindly copy the DPF status.message into the OPI status.message. Implement a reconciliation dictionary that parses common DOCA/DPF string errors and maps them to standard metav1.Condition types (e.g., Reason: DPUHardwareFault, Reason: NetworkPlumbingFailed). This maintains the vendor abstraction for the end-user.

3. Mitigating Lifecycle and State Management Flaws

Relying on standard OwnerReferences is insufficient for cross-namespace operations and robust hardware teardowns.

  • Custom Finalizers (The Mandatory Fix): Because OwnerReferences do not work across namespaces, the OPI Operator must inject a Custom Finalizer (e.g., opi.linuxfoundation.org/dpu-cleanup) onto its own DpuCluster resources.
  • Active Teardown Polling: When a user deletes the DpuCluster, the API server will mark it with a deletionTimestamp, but the Finalizer will prevent actual deletion. The OPI Operatorâ€™s reconcile loop detects this timestamp, issues a DELETE command to the downstream NVIDIA DPUSet, and waits. It actively polls the API server until the DPUSet is completely gone (meaning the DPF Operator has successfully wiped the hardware). Only then does the OPI Operator remove its Finalizer, allowing the DpuCluster to be deleted.
  • Fail-Safe Blocking: If the DPF Operator is crashed or the hardware is unreachable, the Finalizer ensures the DpuCluster remains in a Terminating state indefinitely. While annoying, this is the correct Kubernetes pattern: it prevents the system from silently losing track of un-wiped hardware.

4. Mitigating Control Plane Amplification

To prevent reconciliation storms and protect the API server from etcd bloat, we must severely restrict when the OPI Operator wakes up.

  • Aggressive Event Filtering (Predicates): By default, controller-runtime triggers a reconcile loop for any update to a watched object. The OPI Operator must implement strict Predicates on its Informers:
    • GenerationChangedPredicate: Only wake up if the Spec of the OPI resource actually changed.
    • Custom Status Predicate: When watching the downstream NVIDIA DPUSet, filter out noisy heartbeat updates. Only trigger an OPI reconcile if a specific, meaningful status transition occurs (e.g., transitioning from Provisioning to Ready or Failed).
  • Targeted Caching: Do not cache all resources cluster-wide. Configure the controller-runtime Manager to cache only the specific namespaces or labels where DPF resources are expected to reside, drastically reducing the memory footprint of the OPI Operator.

Review the entire architecture as if you were a maintainer of controller-runtime. Identify any places where the design violates Kubernetes Operator best practices. Suggest improvements while keeping the architecture unchanged.

Stepping into the shoes of a controller-runtime maintainer, I can tell you that while the overarching CRD delegation architecture is sound, the specific mitigations we just discussed contain a few classic traps. When you build meta-operators, you are putting immense trust in the underlying machinery of the Kubernetes control plane.

If we implement the previous mitigations naively, the operator will fight the controller-runtime framework rather than leveraging it. Here is a critical review of the design flaws from a framework perspective, along with the Kubernetes-native corrections.

1. The Polling Anti-Pattern (Worker Starvation)

The Violation: The previous design suggested that during teardown, the OPI Operator "actively polls the API server until the DPUSet is completely gone."

In controller-runtime, the Reconcile function runs on a limited pool of worker goroutines. If you write a for loop with a time.Sleep() inside your reconciler to wait for the DPF Operator to delete hardware, you are holding that worker hostage. If 10 DPUs are deleted at once, you will exhaust your worker pool, and the entire OPI Operator will freeze, completely unable to process any other cluster events.

The Improvement: You must respect the level-triggered nature of Kubernetes. Never block the thread. Instead of polling, issue the DELETE command to the unstructured DPUSet, and then immediately return a RequeueAfter result.

// Correct controller-runtime pattern for waiting
err := r.Delete(ctx, dpuSet)
if err != nil && !apierrors.IsNotFound(err) {
    return ctrl.Result{}, err
}

if !apierrors.IsNotFound(err) {
    // The DPUSet still exists. Do not poll. 
    // Tell the queue to wake this thread up again in 10 seconds.
    return ctrl.Result{RequeueAfter: time.Second * 10}, nil
}

// If we reach here, the DPUSet is gone. Remove the Finalizer.

2. Cache Panics on Missing CRDs (GVK Discovery)

The Violation: To avoid compile-time coupling, we chose to use unstructured.Unstructured clients. However, when the OPI Operator starts, controller-runtime attempts to establish an Informer (a watch) on the specified GroupVersionKind (GVK).

If a user installs the OPI Operator before installing the NVIDIA DPF CRDs, the API server will return a "no matches for kind" error. By default, controller-runtime will panic and crashloop the manager on startup if a watched CRD does not exist.

The Improvement: Do not register the watch for the NVIDIA DPUSet statically in the SetupWithManager function. Instead, implement a Dynamic Watch. Use the Discovery Client in a background goroutine to periodically check if the NVIDIA CRDs have been installed in the cluster. Only once they are discovered should you dynamically add the watch to the running Manager.

3. Infinite Finalizer Deadlocks (The Zombie Problem)

The Violation: We introduced a custom Finalizer on the DpuCluster to prevent it from deleting until the downstream DPUSet is confirmed destroyed.

What happens if the cluster administrator permanently uninstalls the NVIDIA DPF Operator, or the DPU hardware is physically ripped out of the server? The DPUSet will never be cleanly deleted by the downstream controller. Your OPI Finalizer will block forever, leaving "zombie" DpuCluster resources stuck in a Terminating state permanently, which blocks namespace deletion and frustrates administrators.

The Improvement: Implement a Circuit Breaker or a Time-to-Live (TTL) on the teardown phase. When the deletion timestamp is first set, record that time. If the downstream DPUSet has not successfully terminated after a generous window (e.g., 30 minutes), the OPI Operator should log a massive warning, emit a Kubernetes Event detailing the hardware orphan risk, and forcefully remove its own Finalizer to unblock the cluster.

4. Unstructured Cache Bloat

The Violation: When you instruct controller-runtime to watch an unstructured.Unstructured object, it caches the raw JSON payload of every single instance of that object in the cluster into memory. Because you are not using strongly typed Go structs, the cache footprint is significantly larger. Over thousands of nodes, this will cause the OPI Operator to consume massive amounts of RAM.

The Improvement: Utilize controller-runtime's Cache Selectors. When setting up the Manager, restrict the cache so it only stores DPUSet objects that possess a specific label injected by the OPI Operator (e.g., managed-by: opi-operator). This ensures your controller ignores any DPUSet resources manually created by administrators outside of the OPI ecosystem, keeping the memory footprint lean and tightly scoped.

By embracing the event-driven queue and protecting your manager's cache and finalizers, you transform the OPI Operator from a fragile polling script into a robust, scalable Kubernetes citizen.

Explain how each principle is reflected in the proposed architecture.

The CRD delegation architecture (Approach 1), especially with the framework-level mitigations we discussed, is a textbook example of modern, cloud-native system design. By building a "Meta-Operator," we rely on the Kubernetes control plane to enforce these classic software engineering principles.

Here is how each architectural principle is actively reflected in the design of the OPI-to-DPF integration.

1. Separation of Concerns

The Principle: Different parts of a system should manage distinct, non-overlapping domains. How it's reflected: We divided the problem into two distinct layers: orchestration and execution. The OPI Operator is solely concerned with the user-facing domainâ€”parsing standardized infrastructure requests and mapping generic intent. The NVIDIA DPF Operator is solely concerned with the silicon domainâ€”managing PXE boot sequences, pushing BFB images over RShim, and establishing Host-Based Networking. Neither operator attempts to do the otherâ€™s job.

2. Loose Coupling

The Principle: Components should have minimal knowledge of and dependencies on each other. How it's reflected: The two operators share zero code and do not communicate directly.

  • No Go Imports: By utilizing unstructured.Unstructured dynamic clients, the OPI Operator does not import NVIDIAâ€™s Go modules, breaking compile-time coupling.
  • No Direct RPC: They do not speak over a direct network socket or gRPC connection. They only communicate indirectly by writing and reading data from the Kubernetes API Server (etcd). If the DPF Operator goes offline, the OPI Operator continues running without throwing network connection errors.

3. Vendor Abstraction

The Principle: Hiding the complex, proprietary implementation details of a subsystem behind a generalized interface. How it's reflected: The end-user (or the CI/CD pipeline) never writes a DPUSet or a DPUCluster. They exclusively write the open-standard DpuCluster YAML. The OPI Operator absorbs the complexity of translating standard parameters into the proprietary flags required by DOCA. To the user, an NVIDIA BlueField card and an Intel Mount Evans card look identical at the API surface level.

4. Declarative Control

The Principle: Stating what the desired outcome is, rather than explicitly commanding how to achieve it (imperative). How it's reflected: The OPI Operator does not send imperative commands to the DPF Operator like "Flash firmware version X now." Instead, it submits a declarative document (DPUSet YAML) to the API Server stating: "I want a BlueField DPU running version X." The DPF Operator's internal continuous reconciliation loop figures out the steps required to make reality match that document. Server-Side Apply (SSA) further enforces this by declaring precise ownership of specific fields.

5. Single Responsibility

The Principle: A module, class, or service should have only one primary reason to change. How it's reflected:

  • If NVIDIA changes how BlueField cards boot over PCIe, only the DPF Operator needs to be updated.
  • If the Linux Foundation updates the OPI schema to include a new standardized networking parameter, only the OPI Operator needs to be updated. By avoiding the monolithic "Embedded Client" approach, we ensure that a bug in vendor-specific hardware management cannot crash the generic, cluster-wide infrastructure orchestration layer.

6. Extensibility

The Principle: A system should be designed to allow the addition of new capabilities without modifying existing core logic. How it's reflected: The architecture is inherently pluggable. If tomorrow we need to support AMD Pensando DPUs, we do not need to rewrite the OPI Operator's core reconciliation loop. We simply write a new schema translator module within the OPI Operator that maps DpuCluster fields to the AMD Operatorâ€™s CRDs. The generic logic (watching the OPI API, managing finalizers, parsing statuses) remains completely untouched.

7. Event-Driven Architecture

The Principle: System components react to state changes (events) produced by other components rather than continuously polling for state. How it's reflected: The entire system operates on Kubernetes Informers and Workqueues.

  • When the user submits a DpuCluster, the API Server fires an ADD event, waking up the OPI Operator.
  • When the OPI Operator submits a DPUSet, the API Server fires an ADD event, waking up the DPF Operator.
  • When the hardware finishes provisioning, the DPF Operator patches the status, firing an UPDATE event that bubbles back up to the OPI Operator. Because we rely on strict predicates and generation-filtering, the operators sit completely idle (consuming almost zero CPU) until a relevant event flows through the system.

1. Executive Summary

1.1 Abstract

This document outlines the architectural design for integrating NVIDIA BlueField Data Processing Unit (DPU) support into the Open Programmable Infrastructure (OPI) Operator. To avoid replicating complex, silicon-level provisioning logic, we propose a Meta-Operator (CRD Delegation) architecture. The OPI Operator will act as a higher-order orchestrator, translating vendor-agnostic OPI Custom Resources into NVIDIA DOCA Platform Framework (DPF) Custom Resources. This design strictly adheres to Kubernetes controller-runtime best practices, ensuring high scalability and decoupled lifecycle management.

1.2 Architectural Principles

  • Separation of Concerns: The OPI Operator handles user intent and standardization; the DPF Operator handles hardware execution.
  • Loose Coupling: The integration uses unstructured dynamic clients, eliminating compile-time dependencies on NVIDIA's Go modules.
  • Declarative Control: System state is managed via Server-Side Apply (SSA) rather than imperative API calls.
  • Vendor Abstraction: End-users interface exclusively with OPI standards, shielded from proprietary DOCA schemas.
  • Event-Driven Reactivity: Utilizing level-triggered controllers with strict predicates to minimize API server load.

2. Problem Statement & Background

2.1 Context

Modern data centers utilize DPUs and IPUs to offload infrastructure tasks (SDN, NVMe-oF, Zero-Trust Security) from the host CPU. The OPI project provides a vendor-neutral standard for these workloads. Simultaneously, NVIDIA provides the highly specialized DPF Operator, optimized for BlueField hardware and the DOCA software stack.

2.2 Core Challenges

Integrating BlueField into OPI introduces a build-vs-buy dilemma. Rebuilding the mechanics required to flash BlueField firmware (BFB), orchestrate RShim over PCIe, and initialize Kamaji tenant clusters within the OPI Operator is prohibitively complex and replicates existing NVIDIA efforts. We must bridge the open standard with the proprietary executor without introducing tight systemic coupling or compromising cluster stability.

3. Goals and Non-Goals

3.1 Goals

  • Maximize the reuse of the upstream NVIDIA DPF Operator for all hardware-level lifecycle events.
  • Preserve strict vendor abstraction at the user-facing API boundary (DpuCluster).
  • Maintain a highly resilient, memory-efficient control plane that passes standard controller-runtime audits.

3.2 Non-Goals

  • Writing direct hardware-level drivers, PXE boot managers, or DOCA telemetry agents within the OPI Operator.
  • Proposing modifications to the internal APIs, release cycles, or proprietary mechanics of the NVIDIA DPF Operator.

4. Proposed Architecture: CRD Delegation Pattern

4.1 Topology Overview

The architecture implements a declarative proxy pattern. The Kubernetes API server acts as the central integration bus. The OPI Operator functions as a "Meta-Controller." It registers a watch on standard OPI Custom Resources. Upon reconciliation, it performs a deterministic transformation of the OPI spec into an NVIDIA DPF spec (DPUSet / DPUCluster). It then applies this unstructured payload to the cluster. The downstream DPF Operator, completely unaware of OPI, detects its native resources and provisions the hardware.

5. Detailed Design & Control Loop Mechanics

5.1 Dynamic and Unstructured Schema Mapping

To avoid dependency rot, the OPI Operator will strictly avoid importing github.com/NVIDIA/deployment-operator.

  • Mechanic: The operator uses client.Client configured for unstructured.Unstructured.
  • Translation Registry: A translation module parses the generic DpuCluster parameters. It queries the cluster's Discovery Client to determine the installed DPF API version (e.g., v1alpha1 vs v1beta1) and routes the payload to the corresponding JSON schema generator.
  • Feature Gating: If a user requests a networking mode unsupported by the detected DPF version, a Validating Admission Webhook rejects the DpuCluster synchronously, preventing silent downstream failures.

5.2 Server-Side Apply (SSA) & Conflict Management

Because the generated DPUSet resources exist in the cluster, they are vulnerable to out-of-band administrative edits.

  • Mechanic: The OPI Operator applies downstream resources using Kubernetes Server-Side Apply (PatchType: Apply).
  • Field Manager: The operator registers itself as the Field Manager (manager: opi-controller) for the generated spec fields. If an administrator manually modifies an OPI-owned field, the API server will reject the change (unless forced), preventing infinite reconciliation loops between the admin and the controller.

5.3 Asynchronous Status Synchronization & Error Abstraction

The user must see standard Kubernetes states, not proprietary DOCA errors.

  • Mechanic: The OPI Operator maintains a dynamic watch on the generated DPUSet resources.
  • Error Translation: When the DPF operator writes a hardware fault to the DPUSet status, the OPI reconciler intercepts the event. It runs the NVIDIA-specific status.message through an internal translation dictionary, mapping known DOCA errors to standardized metav1.Condition types (e.g., Type: Provisioned, Status: False, Reason: HardwareFault).

5.4 Lifecycle Orchestration & Non-Blocking Teardown

OwnerReferences fail across namespaces and cannot guarantee complete hardware wiping.

  • Mechanic: The OPI Operator injects a custom finalizer (opi.linuxfoundation.org/dpu-cleanup) on the DpuCluster upon creation.
  • Teardown Flow: Upon deletion, the OPI Operator catches the deletionTimestamp. It issues a DELETE command to the downstream DPUSet.
  • Worker Preservation: It does not poll. It returns ctrl.Result{RequeueAfter: 15 * time.Second}. The worker thread is immediately freed. Subsequent loops check if the DPUSet responds with apierrors.IsNotFound. Only then is the OPI finalizer removed.

6. Performance, Scalability, and Resilience

6.1 Control Plane Optimization & Event Filtering

Unstructured caches can consume massive amounts of RAM if unbounded.

  • Cache Selectors: The controller-runtime Manager will be configured with a LabelSelector. The cache will only store DPUSet resources bearing the label app.kubernetes.io/managed-by: opi. This shields the OPI Operator from caching manually provisioned NVIDIA DPUs.
  • Event Predicates: The controller implements a GenerationChangedPredicate. The reconciler will drop UPDATE events where only the resource version changed, preventing reconciliation storms during routine cluster telemetry updates.

6.2 Circuit Breaking & Failure Domains

To prevent "zombie" resources (where a failed hardware wipe permanently blocks namespace deletion):

  • TTL Circuit Breaker: The teardown loop records the initial deletion timestamp. If the downstream DPUSet fails to terminate within a configurable Time-to-Live (TTL, default 30 minutes), the OPI Operator emits a Warning Kubernetes Event ("Hardware wipe timeout; forcefully decoupling") and aggressively removes its finalizer.

6.3 Security and RBAC Constraints

The OPI Operator requires elevated permissions to manipulate unstructured resources, but must adhere to least privilege.

  • Role Bindings: ClusterRoles will be strictly limited to CREATE, PATCH, UPDATE, GET, LIST, WATCH, DELETE operations exclusively on the dpusets.mellanox.com and dpuclusters.mellanox.com API groups. The Operator will not require host-level networking, privileged security contexts, or node-agent daemonsets.

7. Alternatives Considered & Rejected

7.1 Alternative A: Southbound OPI Node Shim (Node-Level Translation)

  • Design: Deploying an OPI gRPC agent on every node that translates OPI standard RPCs into local DPF execution commands.
  • Rejection Rationale: High operational complexity. Bridging synchronous gRPC calls with Kubernetes' asynchronous, level-triggered control loops introduces severe race conditions. It also requires managing mTLS certificates for hundreds of node-level agents.

7.2 Alternative B: Embedded DPF Client (Shared Reconciler)

  • Design: Importing the github.com/NVIDIA/deployment-operator module directly into the OPI Operator, effectively running both reconciliation loops in a single binary.
  • Rejection Rationale: Violates the Single Responsibility Principle. This creates brittle compile-time dependency rot and forces the generic OPI Operator to require the massive RBAC permissions needed by the NVIDIA hardware executor. A panic in the vendor-specific code would crash global, multi-vendor orchestration.

8. Open Questions & Risks

  1. Schema Validation Drift: How frequently does NVIDIA introduce breaking API changes in minor releases of the DPF Operator, and what is our SLA for updating the OPI internal translation dictionary?
  2. Kamaji Network Partitions: Testing is required to validate DPF Operator finalizer behavior when the secondary tenant control plane (Kamaji) becomes partitioned from the primary management cluster during a teardown event.

Currently, these two architectures operate in isolated, parallel domains with fundamentally different control plane philosophies.

2.1 Current OPI Operator Architecture

The OPI Operator is designed with a strict top-down, vendor-agnostic philosophy. It assumes that the underlying infrastructure processing units (IPUs/DPUs) conform to a standardized set of gRPC APIs for lifecycle management, storage, and networking.

  • Control Plane Surface: Users interact with generic Custom Resources, primarily DpuCluster (for defining fleet-wide provisioning intent) and DpuNodeState (for observing actual hardware capabilities).
  • Execution Mechanic: In a standard OPI deployment, the operator reconciles these generic CRs and attempts to communicate with a generic node-level daemon or directly with the card via OPI standard gRPC endpoints.
  • Design Limitation: The OPI Operator inherently lacks silicon-specific drivers. It expects the hardware to be in a pre-bootstrapped state where it can already accept generic network RPCs.

2.2 Current NVIDIA DPF Operator Architecture

The NVIDIA DPF Operator is a highly specialized, bottom-up architecture designed exclusively to orchestrate the lifecycle of BlueField silicon and the proprietary DOCA software stack.

  • Control Plane Surface: It utilizes a deep array of proprietary Custom Resources, including DPUSet (managing a fleet of cards), DPUCluster, and DPUService (for Service Function Chaining).
  • Execution Mechanic: The DPF Operator manages silicon at the lowest possible level. It uses the RShim driver over the host's PCIe bus to force-push BlueField Boot (BFB) OS images directly onto the card. Furthermore, it often deploys Kamaji to run a secondary "tenant" Kubernetes control plane exclusively for the DPUs, and configures complex Host-Based Networking (HBN) rules.
  • Design Limitation: It is deeply proprietary. It does not speak OPI gRPC standards natively during the bootstrapping phase, and its CRDs are strictly coupled to NVIDIA's internal schemas.

2.3 The Integration Gap

The integration gap driving this proposal lies in the bootstrapping phase and hardware initialization barrier.

The OPI Operator expects to manage a standardized device over a network; however, a raw NVIDIA BlueField card requires complex, proprietary PCIe-level bootstrapping (via RShim and BFB flashing) before any network-facing OPI API can even be initialized on the card.

Currently, a platform engineer cannot use the OPI DpuCluster resource to provision a raw BlueField card because the OPI Operator does not know how to flash a BFB image. Conversely, if the engineer uses the DPF Operator to flash the card, they break vendor abstraction and lock their infrastructure-as-code pipelines into NVIDIA's proprietary DPUSet API. This architectural divide requires a control-plane bridge that preserves the OPI API surface while safely delegating the proprietary hardware bootstrapping to the DPF execution engine.

4.3 Component Responsibilities and Boundaries

To guarantee loose coupling and prevent systemic fragility, this architecture strictly defines the inputs, outputs, and ownership boundaries for each module in the control plane. No component is permitted to bypass these boundaries.

4.3.1 Kubernetes API Server (The Integration Bus)

The API Server acts as the singular integration point and state store for the entire architecture, completely decoupling the operators from one another.

  • Responsibilities: Persisting desired and actual state (via etcd), broadcasting state changes, enforcing Role-Based Access Control (RBAC), and resolving concurrent modification conflicts.
  • Inputs: Declarative REST payloads (manifests) from platform engineers and operators.
  • Outputs: Asynchronous watch events (ADD, UPDATE, DELETE) dispatched to registered informers.
  • Ownership Boundary: Owns the definitive source of truth for the cluster. It remains entirely agnostic to the domain logic of both OPI and NVIDIA.

4.3.2 OPI Operator (The Meta-Controller)

The OPI Operator serves as the high-level orchestrator and the sole interface for the end-user.

  • Responsibilities: Managing the lifecycle of vendor-agnostic infrastructure, enforcing cross-namespace garbage collection via custom finalizers, and surfacing unified infrastructure health metrics to the user.
  • Inputs: DpuCluster Custom Resources created by the user; watch events indicating status changes in downstream NVIDIA resources.
  • Outputs: Generated, vendor-specific NVIDIA Custom Resources; normalized status updates patched back to the parent DpuCluster.
  • Ownership Boundary: Owns the user-facing DpuCluster API and the orchestration lifecycle. It explicitly does not own hardware execution, node-level agents, or network sockets.

4.3.3 Translation Layer (OPI Internal Module)

Operating purely in-memory within the OPI Operator, this module acts as the schema bridge between the open standard and the proprietary ecosystem.

  • Responsibilities: Decoupling the OPI control loop from NVIDIA schema versions, translating abstract infrastructure intent into highly specific hardware configurations, and abstracting proprietary telemetry.
  • Inputs: Generic DpuCluster specifications; raw, vendor-specific status messages and error codes from the DPF ecosystem.
  • Outputs: Dynamically generated, version-compliant NVIDIA Custom Resource payloads; standardized Kubernetes metav1.Condition objects.
  • Ownership Boundary: Owns the schema mapping and feature-gating logic. It is strictly isolated from network clients, API server communication, and hardware.

4.3.4 NVIDIA DPF Operator (The Execution Controller)

Treated as a secure, "black box" execution engine, this operator handles all silicon-level complexities.

  • Responsibilities: Bootstrapping bare-metal BlueField hardware, pushing OS images over the PCIe bus, establishing Host-Based Networking (HBN), and managing secondary tenant control planes.
  • Inputs: DPUSet or DPUCluster Custom Resources (generated by the OPI Operator); telemetry and state data from local node agents.
  • Outputs: Imperative hardware execution commands (e.g., PXE boot triggers, RShim transfers); status subresource patches sent to the Kubernetes API Server.
  • Ownership Boundary: Owns the absolute lifecycle of the physical BlueField silicon and its localized host environment. It operates with complete ignorance of the upstream OPI context or any higher-level orchestration.

5. Detailed Design & Control Loop Mechanics

The core architectural challenge of a Meta-Operator is maintaining a robust, vendor-neutral control plane while manipulating proprietary, vendor-specific objects downstream. This section defines the interaction patterns and state machines required to safely execute this delegation.

5.1 API Decoupling and the Anti-Corruption Layer

To ensure the OPI Operator remains resilient to NVIDIAâ€™s internal release cycles, the architecture implements an Anti-Corruption Layer (ACL) between the standardized intent and the proprietary execution.

  • Dynamic Payload Generation: The OPI Operator must strictly avoid compile-time dependencies on downstream NVIDIA schema definitions. Instead of statically compiled structures, the OPI Operator generates dynamic, schema-less payloads at runtime.
  • Version Discovery: The ACL acts as a translation registry. Before generating a downstream payload, it queries the clusterâ€™s API server to discover the currently installed version of the DPF Operator (e.g., v1alpha1 vs. v1beta1). It then maps the generic DpuCluster parameters to the specifically discovered API schema.
  • Policy Enforcement: If a platform engineer requests a standardized feature that is not supported by the underlying BlueField hardware or the installed DPF version, the ACL operates as a policy gate. It synchronously rejects the request at the admission layer, ensuring that incompatible configurations are never persisted to the cluster.

5.2 State Ownership and Conflict Resolution

Because the OPI Operator generates native resources that exist inside the cluster, those resources are vulnerable to out-of-band modifications by cluster administrators or other automation tools, creating the risk of "split-brain" reconciliation loops.

  • Declarative Field Ownership: The architecture dictates that the OPI Operator does not own the entire downstream DPUSet object; it only owns specific fields (e.g., target image version, network mode).
  • API-Level Conflict Management: To enforce this boundary, the integration relies on Kubernetes Server-Side Apply (SSA) mechanics. By explicitly declaring itself as the manager of specific fields, the API server acts as the arbiter. If an administrator alters an OPI-managed field, the OPI Operator deterministically reverts it. If an administrator alters an unmanaged field (e.g., a proprietary NVIDIA debugging flag), the OPI Operator ignores it, allowing coexistence without triggering infinite reconciliation wars.

5.3 Telemetry Abstraction and Status Bubbling

In a delegated architecture, status synchronization must flow strictly bottom-up, completely asynchronously. The end-user must observe a unified, vendor-agnostic health state, regardless of the underlying hardware errors.

  • Asynchronous Observation: The OPI Operator maintains a passive observation watch on the downstream DPF resources. It never polls the hardware or the DPF Operator directly.
  • The Error Normalization Engine: When the DPF Operator writes a hardware fault to the DPUSet status (often a proprietary string like a specific DOCA telemetry failure), the OPI Operator intercepts this state change. The Operator runs the proprietary error through an internal normalization engine, mapping vendor-specific faults to generalized infrastructure concepts.
  • Standardized Surfacing: The normalized state is then patched onto the parent DpuCluster resource using standard Kubernetes metav1.Condition structures (e.g., Type: Provisioned, Status: False, Reason: HardwareFault).

5.4 Distributed Lifecycle Orchestration and Teardown

Standard Kubernetes garbage collection (OwnerReferences) is insufficient for this architecture due to cross-namespace boundary restrictions and the requirement for guaranteed hardware sanitization before logical resource deletion.

  • Distributed Lifecycle Locks (Finalizers): The OPI Operator asserts a custom finalizer on the DpuCluster immediately upon creation. This lock guarantees that the user-facing resource cannot be logically deleted until the physical BlueField hardware is confirmed wiped.
  • Non-Blocking State Machine: Teardown is orchestrated as an asynchronous state machine. When a deletion request is received, the OPI Operator issues a declarative deletion intent to the downstream DPF resource. Crucially, the OPI Operator must immediately release its execution thread after issuing the command.
  • Event-Driven Confirmation: The OPI control loop remains dormant until the API Server broadcasts a confirmation event that the downstream DPF resource has been successfully purged by the DPF Operator. Only upon receiving this definitive, event-driven confirmation does the OPI Operator release its lifecycle lock, allowing the parent infrastructure resource to terminate.

5. Communication Contracts and Interface Boundaries

To guarantee system stability and prevent race conditions between the two independent control loops, the integration relies on strict communication contracts. The OPI Operator and the NVIDIA DPF Operator never communicate directly via network sockets or remote procedure calls (RPC). Instead, the Kubernetes API Server acts as an asynchronous, declarative integration bus.

The following contracts define the boundaries, authorities, and handover mechanisms between the standardized OPI domain and the proprietary NVIDIA execution domain.

5.1 Resource Ownership and Authority

The architecture enforces a strict demarcation of resource authority to prevent "split-brain" management.

  • User Intent Authority (OPI Operator): The OPI Operator is the sole authoritative owner of the DpuCluster Custom Resource. It receives abstract infrastructure requirements from the user and holds the authority to translate that intent into vendor-specific configurations.
  • Execution Authority (DPF Operator): The NVIDIA DPF Operator is the sole authoritative owner of the physical BlueField hardware and the execution state of the DPUSet Custom Resource.
  • The Contract: The OPI Operator acts as a high-level client to the DPF Operator. It dictates the Desired State (the Spec) of the NVIDIA resources, but it strictly cedes control of the Actual State (the execution logic) to the DPF Operator.

5.2 Status Propagation and Error Abstraction

State synchronization flows exclusively bottom-up. The contract guarantees that the user is never exposed to raw, vendor-specific operational telemetry unless explicitly requested.

  • The Bubbling Mechanism: As the DPF Operator executes physical hardware changes (e.g., flashing the BFB image, initializing Kamaji), it patches its operational state onto the DPUSet status subresource.
  • The Translation Contract: The OPI Operator maintains a passive observation watch on the DPUSet. When a status change occurs, the OPI Operator intercepts it. It is contractually obligated to parse proprietary DOCA error codes and translate them into standard, vendor-agnostic OPI health metrics before patching the parent DpuCluster.
  • Asynchronous Guarantee: If the DPF Operator crashes, the OPI Operator retains the last known state of the hardware, preventing cascading control plane failures.

5.3 Lifecycle and Teardown Responsibilities

Hardware teardown introduces significant risk; logical resources must not be deleted from the cluster if the physical hardware has not been securely sanitized and reset.

  • Provisioning Handover: During creation, the OPI Operator generates the downstream NVIDIA resources and immediately yields control. The DPF Operator assumes responsibility for pushing the OS image and establishing network plumbing.
  • Teardown Handover: During deletion, the contract dictates a distributed, two-phase commit process.
    1. The OPI Operator signals a teardown intent by deleting the downstream DPUSet resource, but applies a logical lock (a finalizer) to the parent DpuCluster to prevent its removal.
    2. The DPF Operator intercepts the deletion intent, executes the hardware wipe, and finally deletes the DPUSet.
    3. Only upon observing the total cryptographic removal of the DPUSet does the OPI Operator release its lock, concluding the lifecycle contract.

5.4 API Version Compatibility and Graceful Degradation

Because OPI is an open standard and DPF is a proprietary product, their release cycles are entirely decoupled. The architecture establishes an Anti-Corruption Layer to govern version mismatches.

  • Dynamic Discovery: The OPI Operator must never assume the API version of the underlying DPF Operator. Upon initialization, it queries the cluster to dynamically discover the supported NVIDIA API schema (e.g., v1alpha1 versus v1beta1).
  • The Degradation Contract: If a platform engineer requests a standardized OPI networking feature that the installed version of the DPF Operator cannot execute, the OPI Operator must fail synchronously and deterministically. It rejects the generic request at the admission layer with a clear incompatibility error, ensuring that unsupported configurations are never passed downstream to the execution engine.

9. Conclusion

The CRD Delegation architecture represents the most pragmatic and robust approach to integrating NVIDIA BlueField hardware into the Open Programmable Infrastructure (OPI) ecosystem. By operating as a higher-order Meta-Operator, the OPI control plane preserves its strictly vendor-agnostic API surface while fully leveraging the mature, silicon-level execution logic already built into the proprietary NVIDIA DPF Operator.

This strict separation of concernsâ€”where OPI owns the declarative intent and NVIDIA owns the physical executionâ€”ensures that our core architecture remains decoupled, highly scalable, and immune to compile-time dependency rot.

However, this decoupling trades hardware complexity for state-management complexity. Acting as a translation proxy requires rigorous engineering discipline. The system relies heavily on advanced Kubernetes API mechanicsâ€”specifically Server-Side Apply for conflict resolution, custom finalizers for distributed teardowns, and an Anti-Corruption Layer to mitigate downstream schema drift. Mismanagement of these mechanics risks creating "zombie" resources or endless reconciliation loops.

Ultimately, this architecture serves as a strategic bridge. As the OPI standard gains wider industry adoption, the long-term expectation is that hardware vendors will natively expose OPI-compliant gRPC endpoints directly from their silicon bootstraps. When that convergence occurs, the necessity for intermediate CRD translation will naturally phase out. Until then, this design ensures that platform teams can orchestrate multi-vendor DPU infrastructure today without sacrificing the declarative, event-driven ethos of Kubernetes.

// VendorAdapter groups the translation interfaces for a specific vendor (e.g., NVIDIA)
// by embedding the specialized interfaces.
type VendorAdapter interface {
	VersionDiscoverer
	IntentTranslator
	StatusNormalizer
	LifecycleManager
}

This single change ensures that your code remains 100% compatible with both the architectural design and Go best practices, giving you a robust foundation for integration.
